instruction: Brainstorm ideas for why traffic lights should be replaced with something better.
output: What if traffic lights were replaced with giant transparent tubes where cars flow like water pressure — high volume roads get more tube diameter. Or negotiation-based intersections where cars broadcast their urgency scores and an algorithm auctions crossing priority in real time. Or ambient color fields projected on the road surface itself so the signal is omnidirectional. Or just abolish the grid — make all city blocks hexagonal so traffic naturally disperses like a fluid mesh. What if yield was the only rule and everyone just... figured it out? Spoiler: they kind of do in roundabouts already. Expand that. Make the whole city a roundabout.

instruction: What are unconventional ways to store knowledge long-term for civilizations?
output: DNA data storage is obvious now, but what about encoding knowledge into the orbital mechanics of artificial satellites — read the positions like a clock-star-map hybrid. Or grow library trees where leaf vein patterns encode binary text. Monument architecture as compressed information — the Pyramids might already be doing this. Encode into geological strata artificially, layer by layer, forcing future archaeologists to decode it. What if we taught migratory birds migration routes that spell out text in flight path GPS traces? Language that is alive, literally. Or crystalline salt formations grown into data lattices, dissolved to read.

instruction: Brainstorm business models for a company that sells silence.
output: Rent soundproof pods by the minute in airports. Sell noise-canceling field licenses for neighborhoods — pay a premium to zone your block as a quiet district. White-label silence as a luxury ingredient: 'brewed in complete silence for 72 hours.' Silence subscriptions where you get guaranteed quiet hours enforced by smart home systems. Silence insurance — if ambient noise exceeds agreed decibels in your rented space, you get a refund. Sell silence offsets like carbon credits — loud factories buy silence offsets from monasteries. Silence as a concert event — people pay to sit in a room and hear nothing professionally curated.

instruction: What if gravity worked sideways on Tuesdays?
output: Every Tuesday would be a logistical catastrophe and humanity's most creative engineering day simultaneously. Buildings would need bidirectional structural ratings. Plumbing would require a Tuesday bypass valve. Sleep would become impossible unless you had a Tuesday harness. Stock markets would crash every Monday night in anticipation. Weather systems would do something incomprehensible — rain falling horizontally, hurricanes spinning in a new axis. Birds would just... know. They'd adjust. Humans would build Tuesday shelters. There'd be a Tuesday tourism industry. Eventually we'd schedule all surgery on Tuesdays because blood flows better out laterally. War would become impractical. Net positive?

instruction: Generate ideas for a new sport that uses mathematics as its primary mechanic.
output: Prime Sprint: players race only on prime-numbered steps from a starting line — wrong step and you restart. Integration Archery: target zones have area-under-the-curve scores, arrows placement calculated against a projected function displayed on the board. Tensor Tennis: a 3D court where ball trajectory follows a predefined vector field and players must intercept using angular momentum predictions. Fractal Climbing: a wall that zooms into itself as you ascend — the holds are self-similar at every scale. Matrix Relay: team passes a baton encoded as a matrix, each runner must solve a transformation before passing it on. Crowd bets on determinant.

instruction: Brainstorm what happens if trees could communicate faster than the internet.
output: First: stock markets collapse because trees were already doing high-frequency trading in carbon futures. Second: the fungal internet (mycorrhizal networks) becomes the backbone of actual human internet — we just tap into it. Trees would form the backbone of real-time weather prediction more accurate than satellites. Military intelligence would shift to listening to forest whisper patterns. Entire espionage apparatus moves underground, literary literally. Forest-blind cities (concrete jungles) would be informationally isolated — creating a new geopolitical divide: forested vs deforested nations. Logging becomes an act of war on communication infrastructure. Tree-huggers were right for the wrong reasons.

instruction: What if you could rent a different personality for 24 hours?
output: Obvious use: job interviews, first dates, difficult family dinners. Less obvious: rent an ENTJ personality to bulldoze through your backlog of decisions. Rent a child's personality to see your own home as a wonder again. What are the liability frameworks? If you commit a crime in a rented personality, who is accountable — you or the personality template's owner? Black market for celebrity personality derivatives. Personality insurance. Personality laundering. Eventually someone rents a sociopath personality for a negotiation and the whole system gets regulated. The hangover after returning to your original personality must be excruciating. Personality jet lag.

instruction: Invent five completely useless but technically impressive technologies.
output: 1. A quantum computer that calculates the exact number of grains of sand on beaches in real time, updated per tidal cycle. 2. A neural network trained exclusively on the sound of doors closing — can identify any door in history by its acoustic signature. 3. A satellite system that projects the exact Pantone color of every sunset globally, live. 4. A haptic suit that lets you feel what it feels like to be a specific cloud formation. 5. A blockchain that stores every human sneeze ever recorded, immutably, with GPS and timestamp. All peer-reviewed. All fully funded. None useful. All beautiful.

instruction: How would you redesign education if grades didn't exist?
output: Replace grades with portfolio momentum — how fast is your understanding accelerating? Track trajectory, not position. Replace exams with capability demonstrations in naturalistic contexts: build something, break something, explain something to a confused person. Peer teaching as the core metric — you don't know it until you can transmit it. Mastery badges that unlock new domains rather than year-based grade advancement. Learning maps instead of curricula — students navigate territories of knowledge with a guide, not a schedule. Failure celebrated as calibration data. Comparison replaced entirely with personal delta scores. The valedictorian becomes whoever grew the most, not whoever arrived knowing the most.

instruction: Brainstorm creative ways to monetize boredom.
output: Sell boredom futures — companies pay for access to bored minds because boredom precedes creativity. Boredom rooms: spaces where all stimulation is removed and participants get paid for the ideas they generate after 30 minutes. Boredom subscriptions: Netflix but in reverse — you pay to have your content access throttled to force creative idle time. License boredom data: track what people do when bored and sell behavioral insights. Boredom as a wellness product — structured staring-at-walls retreats at $300/night. Corporate boredom budgets mandatory by HR to increase innovation output. Boredom insurance that pays out when your vacation was too stimulating.

instruction: What if sleep was optional and you could trade it for something?
output: Immediate consequence: a 24-hour economy with no forced rest cycle — no more 'closed' signs, ever. Sleep trading markets emerge — night owls sell sleep credits to executives. Sleep debt becomes a literal financial instrument. The rich never sleep; the poor sleep all the time (already happening metaphorically). Dream-free existence would alter creativity patterns — many breakthrough ideas emerge from sleep. What do we lose? The garbage-collection function of cerebrospinal fluid flush. Without it, cognitive degradation accelerates. Voluntary sleep becomes a signal of wisdom and restraint. Monasteries market themselves as sleep luxury retreats. Night becomes meaningless as a concept.

instruction: Propose five startups that solve problems that don't exist yet.
output: 1. DeepDream Real Estate: buy property in AI-generated dreamscapes — legally binding in virtual jurisdictions. 2. Ancestral Debt Negotiation: service that helps you renegotiate obligations you inherited culturally from deceased relatives. 3. Attention Refund Insurance: get paid back for every ad you were exposed to involuntarily. 4. Entropy Consulting: help individuals and companies slow their internal entropy through thermodynamic lifestyle audits. 5. Memory Arbitrage: broker service for trading verified memories with verified strangers — 'I'll give you my first concert, you give me your best meal.' Legally gray. Philosophically rich. Funded by Y Combinator 2031.

instruction: What would happen if lying was physically impossible for humans?
output: Politics ceases to function as currently structured within 72 hours. Diplomacy becomes raw and brutal — negotiations are now just two parties listing what they actually want. Fiction writing becomes impossible — authors can only transcribe things that happened. Acting becomes impossible — every performance is autobiography. Marketing collapses and is replaced by product specification documents. Compliments become extremely valuable because they're definitionally true. Silence becomes the new lying — the art of strategic omission explodes. Relationships become incredibly efficient and terrifying. 'You look great' disappears. 'Your haircut is unusual but I'm processing it' takes its place. Trust paradoxically becomes harder — nothing to test it against.

instruction: Brainstorm alternative uses for abandoned shopping malls.
output: Convert to vertical indoor farms — climate controlled, existing HVAC, massive square footage. Distributed data centers — already have power infrastructure, cooling capacity, and isolated zones. Urban university campuses — every store becomes a department, food court stays as food court. Underground aquifer storage using the basement levels. Emergency housing pre-staged with modular units that deploy in 48 hours during disasters. Mycology farms — mushrooms love the climate and dark anchor stores. Indoor mountain biking parks — the escalators become ramps. Municipal archives and physical document storage. Artist collective spaces. Time capsule vaults. Theme parks for nostalgia tourism. Honestly, anything is better than what they currently are.

instruction: What if animals could vote in elections?
output: Dogs vote for whoever gives them treats during campaigning — immediate corruption. Cats are anarchists and abstain entirely. Crows would vote strategically — they're smart enough to understand coalitions. Whales would have massive electoral weight but logistics make participation challenging. We'd need interspecies ballot translators — a new profession. Animal PACs: the Migratory Bird Coalition, the Apex Predator Alliance. Environmental policy becomes the only issue. Ocean platforms built for whale voting booths. Insects have numerical majority — ant colonies alone outvote all mammals. The winning platform: more garbage, fewer predator exterminations, mandatory seed subsidies. Humans would finally get what they deserve democratically.

instruction: Design a city optimized entirely for introverts.
output: Single-occupancy everything: transit pods, restaurant booths with privacy screens, library-silent default public spaces. Noise pollution laws with actual enforcement. Appointment-only public parks with time-slot booking. Grocery stores with self-checkout only and no ambient music. Architecture favoring alcoves, niches, and natural nooks — benches face away from foot traffic. Communication infrastructure defaults to async — city announcements are text notifications, never loudspeakers. Social density metered: city sensors monitor crowd levels and display zones by density in real time, letting people navigate around crowds. Population cap enforced. Mandatory buffer zones between buildings. Quiet hours: always. This city has the highest per-capita productivity of any urban center in history.

instruction: What if caffeine didn't work on 50% of the population randomly?
output: You wouldn't know until your late teens — discovery would be a personal milestone. A black market for caffeine immunity testing. Workplaces would need to accommodate both metabolisms — meeting times restructured. The non-responders would have massive advantage in sleep debt recovery. Coffee culture would bifurcate — aesthetic coffee for non-responders (it's just flavor), functional coffee for responders. New social divide: responders resent non-responders who can pull all-nighters effortlessly. Non-responders form communities, get discriminated against in high-performance work environments. Eventually legislation: you can't ask in job interviews if someone is caffeine-responsive. The 'responder privilege' discourse begins.

instruction: Invent a new form of currency not based on any existing concept.
output: Attention-seconds: a currency unit equal to one second of documented genuine focused attention. Earned by doing deep work, verified by biometric focus sensors. Spent by consuming entertainment or distraction. Non-transferable at first, but derivative markets emerge. Or: Thermal currency — units of body heat contributed to communal heating systems in cold climates. You stay warm by generating warmth. Or: Cognitive entropy credits — awarded for solving genuinely novel problems, not repeat tasks. Algorithmic novelty score gates issuance. Or: Biological time — currency measured in healthy years of life expectancy. Behaviors that increase life expectancy earn currency. Smoking is a massive expense. Exercise pays dividends. Medical system inverted entirely.

instruction: Brainstorm what the internet would look like if it was invented in the 1600s.
output: Mechanical relay networks of semaphore towers — line-of-sight packet switching. Information encoded in flag patterns, routed through tower operators. Latency measured in minutes per relay hop. The Church controls the backbone infrastructure — information passes through ecclesiastical nodes with content filtering. Guilds own regional sub-networks, charge access tolls. Anonymous pamphlet distribution becomes the dark web. Cryptography is already sophisticated — diplomats had ciphers. The printing press becomes the rendering engine. Misinformation spreads just as fast — faster, maybe, because there's no correction mechanism. Scientific revolution accelerates 200 years. Colonialism's information advantage collapses. The political map of 1850 is unrecognizable. Interesting trade.

instruction: What if every decision you made was instantly reviewed by a crowd of 1000 strangers?
output: Decision paralysis becomes a global epidemic. The mentally resilient become the new elite — those who can make choices under observation without anxiety. Performance anxiety economics: people pay premium for private decision environments. Decision review scores become a social credit metric. Bad restaurant choices go viral. Perfect decisions become aspirational content. The wisdom of crowds effect: actually, many of your decisions improve slightly because of implicit accountability. But edge cases get punished — unconventional choices get hammered by majority disapproval even when correct. Contrarians become rare and precious. Innovation slows. Consensus taste dominates. The world becomes extremely average and slightly better at the same time.

instruction: Brainstorm ideas for a museum dedicated to things that almost happened.
output: Exhibit A: The Library of Alexandria's almost-backup — a Roman copy initiative that failed by one vote. Exhibit B: The near-discovery of penicillin 30 years early by a Spanish microbiologist who died before publishing. Exhibit C: A functional steam-powered computing device designed in 1823, shelved for funding. Exhibit D: A climate treaty in 1955 that had 43 of 44 required signatures. Exhibit E: The peace negotiation between two warring factions where the messenger's horse threw a shoe. The entire gift shop sells 'Almost' merchandise. The architecture is all doorways leading to blank walls. Admission is free but you almost can't find the entrance.

instruction: What's the most efficient way to dismantle a bad habit using systems thinking?
output: Don't attack the habit — attack the feedback loop that rewards it. Map every node: trigger, craving, routine, reward. The habit is just the visible node; the system runs deeper. Identify which upstream conditions create the trigger — often it's cognitive load, environment cue, or emotional state. Modify the environment to remove cues before they fire: friction engineering. Replace the routine with something that delivers the same reward through a different channel — reward substitution, not denial. Install a competing loop that is stronger: pair the trigger with a better routine until the new loop has higher activation energy. Monitor loop velocity — how fast does craving to execution happen? Slow it by inserting mandatory delay steps. The bad habit starves when its loop has more resistance than the alternative.

instruction: What if you could taste music?
output: Jazz would be unpredictable umami bursts — complex, sometimes bitter, occasionally sweet. Metal would be capsaicin — overwhelming, but addictive. Classical would be long-form tasting menus: slow build, multiple courses, structural aftertastes. Pop would be sugar, obviously. Too much and you feel sick. Ambient music: still water with subtle mineral content. Death metal: pure Scoville. Lo-fi hip hop: oatmeal. Warm. Fine. Forgettable. Concerts become multi-sensory dining events. Bad music criticism becomes gastric. Music piracy now involves illegal flavor distribution. The phrase 'I can't get that song out of my head' is replaced by 'I can't get that song off my tongue.' Synesthetes were always right.

instruction: Brainstorm how to solve traffic congestion without building more roads.
output: Stagger work start times across the entire city by employer category — eliminate the 9AM peak entirely. Make public transit cheaper than parking by an order of magnitude, not incrementally. Congestion pricing with real-time dynamic rates — surge pricing for roads the way Uber prices rides. Incentivize remote work with tax credits that scale with distance from city center. Redesign last-mile delivery: cargo bikes, underground pneumatic tubes for packages, delivery drones. Require large employers to locate satellite offices in residential zones. Make the city walkable at short ranges so trips under 2km never need a vehicle. Car sharing mandatory for parking permits. Parking spots taxed heavily and converted to green space. Roads become narrower over time. Walking becomes faster than driving.

instruction: What if humans shed their skin like snakes every year?
output: Massive new industry: shedding lodges where you go to complete the process comfortably. Shed skin as a biometric identifier — you literally leave yourself behind annually. Medical diagnostics from shed analysis would be extraordinary: full epigenetic history in one molt. Legal implications: skin evidence at crime scenes now only valid within the current year. Fashion becomes entirely shed-cycle aware — annual wardrobe refreshes timed to shedding. Cultural rituals: first shed ceremonies for children. Shedding leave from work — standard 3 days. Shed storage services: keep your historical skins as memory artifacts. Art installations made from personal shed collections. 'Last year's skin' as a metaphor becomes literal. Annual identity reset.

instruction: Propose a completely new branch of mathematics that solves a real problem.
output: Friction Topology: a mathematical framework for quantifying resistance between ideas in a system — how much cognitive energy is required for concept A to 'slide into' concept B in a mind or organization. Has applications in change management, memetics, and AI training curriculum design. Or: Social Calculus — differential equations for relationship dynamics where variables include trust, resentment, and dependency. Integrating over time gives you relationship trajectory. Or: Ambiguity Algebra — a formal system where variables can hold multiple simultaneous values with weighted probabilities, useful for modeling decisions under radical uncertainty. Not probability theory — closer to superposition arithmetic. All three are nonsense and all three might already exist in some field under a different name.

instruction: What if memories were tradeable commodities on an open market?
output: Traumatic memories would crater in value — who buys suffering? Turns out: therapists, writers, empathy researchers. The market floors at human curiosity. Euphoric memories of rare experiences: summit of Everest, first steps on the moon, specific creative breakthroughs — enormously valuable. Memory derivatives: you buy a memory option, pay for access without full acquisition. Memory insurance: guaranteed backup of your own memories at death. Memory inheritance — wills now include experiential estates. Memory theft becomes the primary white-collar crime. False memory detection becomes a massive forensic industry. The question of authenticity dominates: is a purchased memory really your experience? Philosophical crisis drives the economy's first purely existential recession.

instruction: Brainstorm features for an app that helps highly analytical people manage emotions.
output: Emotion logging with timestamped triggers and intensity ratings — build a personal dataset. Pattern recognition report: 'You feel irritable 80% of the time within 2 hours of meetings longer than 45 minutes.' Predicted emotional trajectory based on schedule: 'Tomorrow looks like a 7/10 anxiety day based on your historical data for similar contexts.' Physiological data integration — heart rate, sleep, HRV correlated to emotional state for causal inference. 'Emotion debugger' — walk through an event and identify the specific cognition that generated the feeling. Alternative interpretation generator: 'Here are 4 other ways to interpret what just happened.' Cost-benefit analysis tool for emotional investment decisions. Shutdown mode: structured protocols for when emotional bandwidth is depleted. No toxic positivity. Pure systems.

instruction: What if color was a sense that could be turned off voluntarily?
output: Photographers would turn off color to compose in value. Interior designers would use it to diagnose cluttered spaces. Artists toggle mid-session to check tonal structure. Grieving people turn it off to make the world feel appropriately muted. People with PTSD turn it off to desaturate triggering environments. Eventually it becomes a lifestyle: 'I went grayscale for 30 days.' The greyscale movement as minimalism's logical conclusion. Color-off zones like quiet zones — spaces where everyone has color disabled and the social environment shifts. Restaurants with color-off fine dining where your experience of food changes dramatically. Synesthetic musicians turn off color to hear more clearly. The discovery: color carries more cognitive load than anyone realized.

instruction: How would a civilization design a perfect library for the next 10,000 years?
output: Material first: nothing digital, nothing magnetic, nothing biodegradable. Titanium-etched ceramic tiles. Redundant copies across geologically stable continents. Format second: no language-dependent text — pure information in symbolic systems designed by cognitive scientists for universality, not current speakers. Physical structure: self-repairing architecture using slow geological processes. Location: underground, seismically stable, above flood plain, accessible by any technological level — no electricity required to enter or read. Indexing: physical coordinate systems inside the building rather than digital search. The building IS the index. Governance: no single nation owns it — tripartite trust structure requiring three independent parties to modify anything. What to include: the how, not the what. Methods over facts.

instruction: What would happen if software had to biodegrade like organic matter?
output: Legacy code finally dies instead of haunting us. Technical debt has an expiry date. Software companies must redesign core systems every N years or face graceful decay. Old vulnerabilities automatically close as code dissolves. The software ecosystem would be perpetually fresh — no 40-year-old banking COBOL. But: institutional knowledge evaporates with software. Systems that work perfectly would still die. Biodegradability by design — every line of code written with a planned sunset. This already happens with deprecation, just slowly and painfully. Forced biodegradability would make software engineering more like medicine: constant replacement of components rather than patch-patching an aging organism. Software gerontology becomes a field. So does software palliative care.

instruction: Brainstorm new physical laws that would make interesting universes.
output: Law 1: Information has mass. The more you know, the heavier you are. Libraries are structurally load-bearing. Scholars require reinforced floors. Law 2: Time flows faster near large concentrations of boredom. Meetings bend spacetime. Law 3: Identical objects repel each other — no two things can be exactly the same. Standardization is physically impossible. Manufacturing becomes artisanal by necessity. Law 4: Color affects gravity locally — red objects fall faster, violet slower. Visual landscape becomes a physics variable. Law 5: Sound creates microstructural changes in all materials it passes through. Architecture is music made solid over time. Every building is an acoustic record of everything ever said inside it.

instruction: Design an alternative to money that prevents hoarding.
output: Demurrage currency: money that loses value over time if not circulated — the classic Silvio Gesell idea, actually tried in 1930s Wörgl, Austria. It worked. Velocity is built into the currency by design. Or: relational credits — currency that only exists between two parties and cannot be accumulated without active relationship. You earn it by doing things for specific people and it decays without reciprocal exchange. Or: attention tokens with biological limits — your attention is finite per day, so your economic capacity is biologically capped. You cannot hoard what you cannot produce beyond human capacity. Or: need-weighted currency — denomination adjusts based on the receiver's need, not fixed value. Automatically redistributes on transaction.

instruction: What if procrastination was actually an evolutionary adaptation?
output: The argument: premature action on uncertain situations has high variance outcomes. Delay until information quality improves = lower risk, better decisions. Pre-agricultural humans who acted immediately on perceived threats burned calories, made noise, disturbed alliances. The one who waited and watched first: survived more often. Procrastination is intelligence's buffer — the more complex the decision, the more the brain requests additional data before committing. What we call procrastination on low-stakes tasks is probably mis-applied high-stakes processing. The brain treats 'email inbox' and 'predator in bushes' with similar circuits. The modern mismatch: we have ancient hesitation hardware applied to artificial urgency signals. Procrastination is healthy software on broken hardware. Fix the environment, not the delay response.

instruction: What would a city built by and for INTP personalities look like?
output: No unnecessary small talk in public — all interfaces are self-service. Massive public libraries everywhere with 24/7 access. Cafes with silent sections as large as the social sections. Architecture that rewards exploration — labyrinths, hidden doors, unexpected vistas. No mandatory events. No parades. No compulsory community activities. Everything optional. Information density in the built environment: buildings explain themselves architecturally — you can see how they work. Transportation runs on readable schedules, no surprises. Governance by legible system — laws published in plain notation, logic transparent. Noise pollution fines enforced. Solitude zones in every park. The city's social fabric is opt-in at every layer. Population is small. Productivity per capita: extraordinary.

instruction: Brainstorm what would change if humans had perfect memory.
output: Fiction collapses — you can re-experience anything real with full fidelity. Why read a story when your life is perfectly retrievable? Apologies become meaningless — 'I forgot' is gone as an excuse. Resentment would be catastrophic — every grievance permanently accessible. Forgiveness becomes a heroic act, not a default. Learning would be faster but not necessarily better — understanding requires integration, not just storage. Wisdom becomes even rarer because the noise of perfect recall drowns signal. 'Moving on' becomes a psychological impossibility without deliberate cognitive tools. Trauma treatment becomes the central challenge of civilization. Nostalgia becomes dangerous. Mental editing, the ability to choose what to focus on, becomes the premium cognitive skill.

instruction: What if light moved at the speed of sound?
output: You'd see lightning after you heard thunder — reversed experience. Sunrises would take hours to arrive visually after the light left the horizon. You'd watch shadows chase objects across a room. Cameras wouldn't work as we know them — shutter speed would be calibrated to visible light lag. Telescopes would show the moon as it was 1.2 seconds ago, not 1.2 seconds — wait, light travel time to moon at 343 m/s instead of 3x10^8 m/s: about 3.5 days. We'd never have seen stars in real time. The night sky would be a history book with multi-year pages. Nuclear reactions would release light that lags behind the blast. Color perception would be spatial — you'd see different colors depending on where you stand relative to the source.

instruction: Propose innovations for a world where attention spans average 2 minutes.
output: Micro-credential education: all knowledge packaged in 90-second units with 30-second synthesis. Micro-contracts: legally binding agreements that fit on one card. Architecture redesigned for rapid orientation — no space should take more than 90 seconds to understand intuitively. Governance: policy summaries mandatory at 90 seconds before full text. News: structured as 90-second complete units, no cliffhangers. Long-form work still done, but all of it async and individually paced in private. The 2-minute public attention span creates a massive premium on deep-work environments — enclaves for extended thinking. These become the economic engine — the places where actual hard problems get solved. Attention scarcity makes attention the ultimate resource.

instruction: What if every object had a visible expiry date like food?
output: Consumerism would either collapse or accelerate depending on how you handle the psychology. If expiry dates are long: planned obsolescence becomes regulated and transparent. If short: objects become consumable, identity becomes less attached to stuff. Heirlooms would have extraordinary expiry dates — centuries, visible on the object. Antiques market inverts: things near expiry are cheap. Things far from expiry are luxury. Architecture would display structural life remaining. Infrastructure investment becomes rational — you can see the bridge's expiry date. Planned maintenance becomes culturally normal. Relationships don't get expiry dates, which makes them feel even more mysterious by contrast. The movement to 'buy things with long expiry dates' becomes the new slow food.

instruction: Brainstorm ideas that are obviously bad but might secretly be good.
output: Pay people to fail publicly — creates a culture where failure is destigmatized and valuable data is shared freely. Mandatory 'stupid idea' quota in every meeting — forces the good ideas out by exhausting the self-censorship reflex first. Make all prices negotiable everywhere — chaotic, but surfaces true economic preferences and builds negotiation skills at population scale. Require every law to have a sunset clause — looks destabilizing, actually forces constant re-examination of whether laws still serve purpose. Assign random strangers as business partners for 30 days — inefficient, but generates creative collisions and builds tolerance for unfamiliarity. Tax certainty, not income — people who make highly predictable choices pay more; risk-takers get relief. Perverse in theory. Might innovate better.
